items.title: Linear regression loss sensitivity
items.scenario: A model predicts ŷ = w·x + b with mean squared error L(w, b) = (1/n) Σ (w·xᵢ + b − yᵢ)² over n training points. Before running gradient descent, an engineer computes ∂L/∂w and ∂L/∂b at the current (w, b) to see which parameter moves the loss more at this step.
items.explanation: Each partial derivative isolates sensitivity to one parameter: ∂L/∂w aggregates how prediction error changes with slope w; ∂L/∂b does the same for the intercept. Together they form the 2D gradient that drives the weight update.

items.title: Feature sensitivity in a tabular classifier
items.scenario: A fraud model outputs probability p = σ(w₁·amount + w₂·velocity + b). Risk analysts evaluate ∂p/∂amount and ∂p/∂velocity at a specific transaction to explain which input would most increase fraud score if increased slightly.
items.explanation: Partial derivatives with respect to input features measure local sensitivity—useful for interpretability and debugging whether the model responds sensibly to each coordinate.

items.title: Two-parameter toy surface
items.scenario: A student studies f(x, y) = x² + xy + y² at the point (1, 2). They compute ∂f/∂x = 2x + y = 4 and ∂f/∂y = x + 2y = 5, then predict that a small step (Δx, Δy) = (0.1, −0.05) changes f by approximately 4·0.1 + 5·(−0.05) = 0.15.
items.explanation: The linear approximation f(a+Δx, b+Δy) ≈ f(a,b) + (∂f/∂x)Δx + (∂f/∂y)Δy is the same first-order idea behind multivariable Taylor expansions and local loss linearization in optimization.
